research_item: Deterministic Demo and Live AI Reliability
executive_verdict: The critique is substantially correct: KANIT's buildathon demo should not depend on free-form live LLM behavior for its core claim. The impressive claim is not that an LLM can summarize one 8D/CAPA case; it is that the backend can turn multiple quality incidents into the same canonical skill gap, explain the evidence trail, and calculate a readiness score in a repeatable way. For a 3-minute Ford-facing demo, the safest and most credible architecture is controlled live AI plus deterministic product logic: curated synthetic but realistic sample incidents, fixed skill ontology with canonical IDs, strict schema validation, rule-based readiness scoring, visible evidence trace, and fail-closed behavior if the live model does not return a valid result. A demo may use predesigned cases without being fake, but it must not pretend that arbitrary real-world 8D documents are fully solved unless that path has been evaluated. The COPQ proxy and export timeline should be removed from the live 3-minute demo or shown only as roadmap/API capability.
critique_validity: Valid. The critique identifies the main failure mode of AI demos: probabilistic outputs can break the deterministic story. If three semantically identical labels are returned as different strings, pattern mining will fail unless the backend normalizes them to canonical skill IDs. The critique is also right that an unexplained readiness score will not survive an engineering audience; Ford or supplier-quality evaluators will expect a transparent formula, traceable inputs, and auditable reasoning. It is slightly too harsh on curated demo data: using synthetic or anonymized sample data is normal and responsible when the system handles sensitive enterprise or quality data. The key distinction is honesty and architecture. A reliable demo can be deterministic at the workflow level while still showing real AI extraction inside clear boundaries. The demo should show that AI output is constrained, validated, and converted into controlled product signals rather than blindly trusted.
evidence_from_sources: NIST AI RMF says trustworthy AI depends on characteristics such as validity, reliability, accountability, transparency, explainability, privacy, and safety, and defines reliability as the ability to perform as required under expected conditions. NIST also warns that AI systems create reproducibility and testing challenges compared with traditional software. OpenAI's Structured Outputs guidance distinguishes JSON mode from schema adherence and says strict schema-constrained outputs are designed to make model outputs match developer-supplied schemas. LangChain documentation similarly recommends provider-native structured output where available and highlights structured-output validation and error handling. Pydantic AI documentation warns that prompted JSON is often the least reliable approach and that validation/retry is needed. AWS Prescriptive Guidance says evaluation is a critical part of generative AI development, recommends high-quality human-curated evaluation datasets early in the PoC phase, and recommends keeping humans in the loop for critical decisions. Microsoft responsible AI guidance emphasizes reliability, transparency, accountability, and visible risk mitigation for AI agents. Reprise's AI demo guidance states that AI demos are risky because outputs are variable and recommends pre-generated or controlled outputs, synthetic/anonymized industry data, and consistent repeatable storytelling. These sources support a deterministic demo strategy for KANIT: live AI may extract or classify, but scoring, pattern mining, demo state, and final decisions must be constrained and repeatable.
ford_or_automotive_relevance: Ford and automotive supplier-quality contexts are evidence-heavy, not vibes-heavy. Ford's supplier quality portal publicly lists Quality Issue Management, Q1 Requirements, Supplier Improvement Metric, 8D submission/search, online quality training, ePPAP, Ford-specific IATF 16949 requirements, Ford-specific PPAP requirements, MSA, and PPAP checklist resources. IATF's February 2025 notice shows Ford continuing to update customer-specific requirements for IATF 16949 and PPAP, including supplier assessments, special-process tracker links, control-plan expectations, and Level 3/5 PPAP approval requirements. This makes repeatability, traceability, and explainability especially relevant: a Ford-facing jury will likely respect a backend that says 'here is the rule, here is the evidence, here is the canonical skill ID, here is the formula' more than a black-box AI score. Direct evidence about the exact preferences of this buildathon's individual judges is unavailable [uncertain], but the quality-system context strongly favors deterministic, auditable logic.
technical_implication: P0 technical changes are required before frontend polish. First, KANIT needs a fixed skill ontology with canonical IDs, bilingual aliases, definitions, and forbidden free-form labels. The LLM should return only canonical IDs from an enum, and the backend should reject or map any unsupported label. Second, every LLM output used downstream should be parsed into a strict schema and validated with Pydantic or equivalent; invalid outputs should retry once, then fail closed with a visible 'needs human review' state rather than silently falling back to a fake success. Third, readiness scoring must be formula-based and returned with a score breakdown, for example: base 100 minus missing evidence penalties, repeated-gap penalties, unresolved mentor-review penalties, and high-severity penalties. Fourth, demo fixtures should be versioned golden cases with expected canonical skill IDs and expected readiness score ranges. Fifth, the live AI layer should be limited to extraction/summarization/classification, while pattern mining, graph creation, readiness score, and risk level remain deterministic backend code. Sixth, the backend should log model, prompt version, schema version, ontology version, validation result, and evidence trace for demo credibility.
demo_implication: The 3-minute demo should become narrower and more rigorous. Keep the Demo Launcher, Evidence Graph, and Shift Readiness Board. Remove the Export Timeline from the live story unless there is spare time. Remove COPQ/scrap exposure from the live demo because it will look like a calculator without real production cost data. Use three predesigned synthetic 8D/CAPA cases that intentionally contain the same missing effectiveness-verification and weak visual-evidence pattern. Show the raw case snippets briefly, then show that the AI extraction maps all three to the same canonical skill node: effectiveness_verification. On the readiness screen, never show only '48/100'; show '48 = 100 - 30 missing evidence - 20 repeated skill gap - 2 pending mentor review' or the actual implemented formula. Add a small 'Live AI extraction: passed schema validation' indicator if NVIDIA is called live. If the API fails, show 'live extraction failed validation, deterministic golden case loaded for demo continuity' only if this is explicitly labeled; do not pretend it was live.
buildathon_implication: For buildathon scoring, this research changes the priority order. Problem meaningfulness improves when the demo is anchored in a real quality workflow and not generic training. AI integration improves when AI is used for bounded extraction and evidence interpretation rather than arbitrary scoring. Working prototype scoring improves when the same demo can be replayed reliably under time pressure. The best buildathon artifact is therefore not the broadest backend but the most inspectable working slice: 3 cases -> schema-validated AI extraction -> canonical skill ontology -> evidence graph -> formula-based readiness score -> mentor action. Commercial pilot language should be secondary. In the demo, the jury should first believe that the system works, then understand why it matters to Ford quality and training.
recommended_decision: Modify. Keep the overall KANIT Readiness Engine concept, but harden the demo around deterministic reliability. Implement a canonical skill ontology and schema-constrained extraction before building more frontend. Keep live AI only where failure is bounded and visible. Make readiness formula transparent and auditable. Remove COPQ from the 3-minute live demo and keep it in the README as a future decision-support module requiring production cost data. Remove export timeline from the main demo unless it can be shown in under 10 seconds as a technical footnote. Use curated synthetic sample cases and label them as 'anonymized-style demo cases based on automotive quality workflows' rather than pretending they are real Ford data.
implementation_priority: P0. Without deterministic skill normalization, schema validation, and formula explainability, the central demo claim can fail live. P0 tasks: build the ontology/alias layer; force LLM output to canonical skill_id values; add schema validation and failure states; expose readiness score breakdown; create golden demo cases and tests; add regression tests that three differently worded incidents resolve to the same skill_id; add a demo endpoint that seeds the exact 3-case story. P1 tasks: add UI for evidence graph and score breakdown. P2 tasks: COPQ proxy, integration timeline, and broader export stories.
claims_we_can_make: Safe claims: KANIT uses AI for evidence extraction and case interpretation, then converts results into controlled canonical skill signals. KANIT does not rely on arbitrary AI labels for pattern mining; it uses a fixed skill ontology. KANIT's readiness score is formula-based and explainable. KANIT's demo data is synthetic/anonymized-style and designed to show a realistic automotive quality workflow without exposing confidential supplier data. KANIT fails closed when AI output is invalid or uncertain. KANIT is different from ChatGPT because it links multiple incidents to the same canonical skill gap, shows evidence trace, calculates readiness with auditable rules, and requires mentor review before readiness changes are accepted.
claims_to_avoid: Avoid claiming the demo proves production accuracy on arbitrary real Ford 8D cases. Avoid saying the system is Ford-approved, integrated with Ford QIM/SIM/ePPAP, or ready for supplier deployment without security and validation work. Avoid claiming the readiness score is a true prediction of scrap, quality escapes, or employee performance. Avoid saying 'live AI analyzed everything' if the core outputs come from fixtures or cached golden runs. Avoid presenting COPQ exposure as financial truth without production cost, scrap, rework, volume, and escape data. Avoid individual-worker scoring language that sounds punitive or surveillance-driven.
sources: https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/ai/NIST.AI.100-1.pdf - NIST AI Risk Management Framework: trustworthiness characteristics, reliability definition, validation, explainability, reproducibility and testing risks.
https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework - NIST AI RMF landing page and generative AI/critical infrastructure profile context.

https://openai.com/index/introducing-structured-outputs-in-the-api/ - OpenAI Structured Outputs announcement: JSON mode does not guarantee schema adherence; strict structured outputs improve schema reliability.
https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/structured-outputs - OpenAI structured output guidance for schema-constrained outputs.
https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/structured-output - LangChain structured output docs: provider-native structured output, Pydantic schemas, validation, strict mode, and error handling.
https://pydantic.dev/docs/ai/core-concepts/output/ - Pydantic AI output docs: structured output validation, retry, and limitations of prompted JSON/schema-only approaches.

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/gen-ai-lifecycle-operational-excellence/dev-experimenting-quality.html - AWS Prescriptive Guidance: evaluation datasets, validation against human judgment, and human-in-the-loop final gates for critical decisions.

https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-copilot-studio/guidance/maturity-model-security-governance - Microsoft agentic AI maturity guidance: responsible AI risk radar, reliability, transparency, escalation, and concrete risk actions.
https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/ai/principles-and-approach/ - Microsoft Responsible AI principles: reliability, transparency, accountability, privacy, safety, and responsible deployment.

https://www.reprise.com/resources/blog/top-reasons-ai-is-difficult-to-demo-and-what-you-can-do-about-it - AI demo guidance: controlled demos, synthetic/anonymized data, repeatable storytelling, and avoiding live AI fragility.
https://docs.cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/generative-ai/docs/model-reference/evaluation - Google Vertex AI Gen AI Evaluation API: model output evaluation using custom metrics and criteria.

https://web.purinfo.ford.com/supqualport/html/quality.html - Ford Supplier Quality portal: QIM, Q1, SIM, 8D, online quality training, ePPAP, Ford IATF/PPAP requirements, MSA, and PPAP checklist resources.
https://www.iatfglobaloversight.org/news/12-february-2025-ford-csr-update-iatf-16949-ppap/ - IATF notice on Ford 2025 CSR updates for IATF 16949 and PPAP.
